scenario_title: Add then delete a different RRset in the same owner/type
test_rationale: This test isolates the underspecified case where one UPDATE first adds an A record for temp.campus.edu. and then deletes the entire A RRset for that same owner/type. If the server processes update prerequisites and actions as an ordered sequence, the final state is empty and the reply should be NOERROR. If it instead evaluates the UPDATE as a conflicting batch or against the pre-update state, it may reject the message or handle it non-sequentially. The follow-up query makes the final RRset state observable, yielding a binary oracle across implementations.

group_1.behavior: Applies UPDATE operations sequentially; the added A RR is then removed by delete_rrset, so the transaction succeeds with no remaining A RRset.

group_2.behavior: Treats the same-owner/type add-then-delete_rrset combination as a conflicting/invalid update and rejects it, or otherwise does not apply the sequence as ordered instructions.
group_2.post_state: temp.campus.edu. has no A RRset or remains absent

group_3.behavior: Any implementation that accepts the update but leaves the A RRset present would also diverge on post-state.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3 and §7.2 define how UPDATEs are processed, but they do not require a server to interpret multiple update records as strictly ordered sequential mutations versus a set of net effects validated against the pre-update database. RFC 2181 §5.2 reinforces RRset atomicity expectations, yet it does not resolve intra-message conflicts where an RRset is created and then deleted in the same UPDATE. As a result, compliant servers may either apply the add then the delete and return NOERROR, or reject/handle the contradictory transaction differently, producing observable reply and/or post-state differences.
